name: Continuing Bonds
tagline: Stay connected to someone you've lost instead of 'letting go'.

origin.source: Continuing Bonds: New Understandings of Grief (Klass, Silverman & Nickman, 1996)

situations: Pressured to 'move on' before you're ready
Missing a presence in your daily life
Wanting to honor a relationship that continues in memory
summary: Continuing Bonds reframes healthy grief: the goal isn't to sever your connection to the person who died but to transform it into one you can carry forward. You maintain an ongoing inner relationship — through memory, ritual, conversation, and values they gave you. This research-based view replaced older models that demanded 'closure' and 'letting go'.
howItWorks: Older grief models treated detachment as the endpoint of mourning, but research found that bereaved people who maintain a transformed, ongoing bond often adjust as well or better. The connection doesn't keep you stuck; it provides comfort, continuity, and identity. Continuing Bonds lets the relationship change form — from external presence to internalized one — rather than ending, which fits how grief actually works for most people.

steps.instruction: Give yourself permission to keep the relationship, not end it.
Choose ways to stay connected: talk to them, keep an object, mark anniversaries, continue a shared ritual.
Carry forward something of theirs — a value, a phrase, a practice — into how you live.
steps.mantra: The bond changes; it doesn't break.

steps.instruction: Let the connection evolve over time, allowing both grief and comfort to coexist.

whenToUse: When grieving a death or major loss
When others pressure you to 'move on'
Missing someone's presence in daily life
Wanting to honor an ongoing inner relationship
whenNotToUse: If the bond keeps you from any present-day life or relationships
When it becomes avoidance of the reality of the loss
Alongside complicated or traumatic grief, seek professional support
tips: There's no timeline — grief isn't a problem to finish
Rituals and objects make the bond tangible
Talking to the person (aloud or in writing) is normal and healthy
Let the relationship keep changing as you do

evidence.summary: Continuing bonds is an established, research-supported model in contemporary grief studies, validated across cultures and bereavement research.
citations: Klass, D., Silverman, P.R. & Nickman, S.L. (1996). Continuing Bonds. Taylor & Francis.
